summary: Graham argues that much of mainstream non-political news is shaped by PR firms that feed reporters selective truths, creating stories and trends that appear organic but are strategically placed. This symbiosis persists because reporters are overworked and prefer ready-made narratives, while PR firms avoid outright lying to preserve credibility. He contrasts this with the authenticity of online writing and predicts readers will keep migrating to blogs, forcing PR to adapt.
key_points: PR firms supply a large share of traditional media stories by offering true but selective angles that favor clients.
Reporters rely on PR because it is efficient, and definitive, punchy claims make stories feel authoritative even when they are based on shaky estimates.
Trend pieces and “buzz” are often manufactured by coordinated pitches across outlets, making repetition look like a real movement.
Online writing tends to be more authentic because authors write out of personal motivation rather than institutional incentives.
As readers move online, PR will likely try to influence bloggers, but bloggers resist becoming mouthpieces.
money_quotes: Of the stories you read in traditional media that aren't about politics, crimes, or disasters, more than half probably come from PR firms.
A good flatterer doesn't lie, but tells his victim selective truths.
Most people who publish online write what they write for the simple reason that they want to.
The whole tone is bogus. This is the tone of someone writing down to their audience.
key_insight: Mainstream media stories often originate from PR-driven selective truths, while online writing’s authenticity is shifting reader trust and forcing PR to adapt.
